nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; typically found perching among or within the branches of Acropora and other branching SPS coral colonies, generally at shallow depths in lagoon and outer-reef zones.

diet.notes: Generally accepts small meaty foods readily, including frozen mysis shrimp, enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped table shrimp, and prepared carnivore foods. Like other Gobiodon species, has been observed feeding on small invertebrates and zooplankton associated with its host coral in the wild.

avoidWith: boisterous feeders that outcompete small gobies
SPS-only Acropora-dominated displays where polyp nipping would be costly

summary: The Black Clown Goby is a small reef fish from Indo-Pacific reefs, typically reaching around 5 cm. It is generally considered peaceful and beginner-friendly, though it may nip at SPS coral polyps and can release a skin toxin when stressed.
careNotes: A note on taxonomy: the trade name Gobiodon strangulatus does not appear in major taxonomic databases, and the name may be a synonym, a misspelling, or a distinct undescribed form closely related to Gobiodon atrangulatus. The taxonomic identity of this fish should be treated with some caution, and husbandry guidance has been drawn from trade records and the wider Gobiodon genus. Like other Gobiodon species, the Black Clown Goby is coral-commensal, typically perching within or upon branches of Acropora and similar branching SPS corals in the wild and on rockwork in aquariums. It is generally peaceful toward other fish species but has been known to quarrel with its own kind in smaller tanks; keeping a single specimen or a bonded pair is generally advised. Reef compatibility is best described as 'with caution': specimens have been documented nipping SPS coral polyps, and spawning pairs often deposit eggs on coral branches, which may cause localized tissue recession (the coral generally regenerates). Like other Gobiodon species, the Black Clown Goby has been reported to secrete a crinotoxin (a defensive skin toxin) when stressed or dying, which has been known to harm or kill other small fish in confined quarters; prompt removal of any deceased specimen is advised. A tightly-fitted lid is generally recommended, as escape from open-top tanks has been reported across the genus. Spawning in captivity is reported to be relatively common when branching coral is available; Gobiodon species are bidirectional hermaphrodites, meaning either individual in a pair can take on the male or female role, so any two individuals can form a functional mated pair.
breedingNotes: Has been reported to spawn in home aquariums when branching coral is available as a substrate; pairs deposit adhesive demersal eggs on coral branches, which the male tends. Gobiodon species are bidirectional hermaphrodites — either individual in a pair can adopt the male or female role — so sex-matching when purchasing is not required. Rearing planktonic larvae through metamorphosis to settlement is the limiting step; detailed captive-rearing protocols are not widely documented for this species.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Provided care data: scientific name Gobiodon strangulatus, max size 2 in (~5 cm), temperature 72-78 F (22.2-25.6 C), pH 8.1-8.4, dKH 8-12, SG 1.020-1.025, 10 gal minimum tank, Easy care level, Peaceful, Carnivore diet (mysis, brine, frozen carnivore prep foods), Indo-Pacific reefs, reef-safe with caveats on SPS polyp nipping and egg deposition on coral.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: FishBase returned 'Species name is not in the public version of FishBase' for Gobiodon strangulatus — confirming the name is absent from their database. Cross-verification conducted using the closest FishBase-validated congener Gobiodon atrangulatus (Garman, 1903), which confirmed: family Gobiidae, order Gobiiformes, Western Pacific (Japan and Fiji), max 3.5 cm TL male/unsexed, reef-associated among Acropora spp., monogamous mating behavior. Size disagreement: FishBase 3.5 cm vs. LiveAquaria 2 in (~5 cm); adult size filed as 2.5–5.0 cm spanning both. Trade name strangulatus may be a synonym or misspelling; dataStatus set to needs_review for taxonomic verification.

fish.breedingNotes: Has been reported to spawn in home aquariums on branching SPS corals; rearing planktonic larvae through settlement is the limiting step. Bidirectional hermaphrodite — any two individuals can form a functional mated pair.
